summary:
The Western Schism (1378–1417 AD) was a catastrophic division within the Roman Catholic Church when two — and eventually three — rival claimants simultaneously claimed to be the legitimate pope, undermining the Church's authority, generating decades of religious confusion, and accelerating the intellectual movements that would eventually produce the Protestant Reformation. The Schism began when the cardinals who had elected the Italian Urban VI (1378) claimed his behavior was erratic and elected a French antipope, Clement VII, who returned to Avignon — while Urban VI remained in Rome. Both men died and were succeeded by further claimants.

The competing popes excommunicated each other and their followers, creating the absurd situation where devout Christians could not know with certainty which obedience was legitimate — and thus whether their sacraments were valid. European kingdoms split their allegiances largely along political lines (France, Scotland, and Aragon backed the Avignon line; England, the Holy Roman Empire, and most of Italy backed Rome). Attempts to resolve the schism through conciliar authority led to the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which ended the schism by deposing or accepting the resignations of all three claimants and electing Martin V as sole pope. The Council of Constance also burned the reforming theologians Jan Hus (1415) and Jerome of Prague (1416) for heresy — an act that ignited the Hussite Wars in Bohemia and demonstrated both the conciliarist movement's power and its limits. The intellectual tradition of conciliarism (the principle that a general council of the Church holds authority superior to the pope) that the Western Schism generated became an important precedent for Protestant arguments against papal infallibility.
